perturbation.original_text: Voluntary Withdrawal of a Participant Any Participant will have the right to voluntarily withdraw from the Joint Venture at any time. Written notice of intention to withdraw must be served in writing upon the remaining Participants at least Thirty (30) business days prior to the withdrawal date. The voluntary withdrawal of a Participant will result in the dissolution of the Joint Venture. A Dissociated Participant will only exercise the right to withdraw in good faith and will act to minimize any present or future harm done to the remaining Participants as a result of the withdrawal.
perturbation.changed_text: Voluntary Withdrawal of a Participant Any Participant will have the right to voluntarily withdraw from the Joint Venture only after the end of the fixed term mentioned in section 4. Written notice of intention to withdraw must be served in writing upon the remaining Participants at least Thirty (30) business days prior to the withdrawal date. The voluntary withdrawal of a Participant will result in the dissolution of the Joint Venture. A Dissociated Participant will only exercise the right to withdraw in good faith and will act to minimize any present or future harm done to the remaining Participants as a result of the withdrawal.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction because section 27 now states a participant can only withdraw after the end of the fixed term stated in Section 4, while Section 4 defines the Joint Venture as fixed term beginning November 27, 2018 and ending November 30th, 2019 or as otherwise provided in this Agreement, and Section 27 contradicts the phrase "at any time" of section 27. It introduces uncertainty about when a participant can actually withdraw. It is now contradictory because one section states that they may at any time but then another states they may only withdraw after the end of the fixed term.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Joint Venture is a fixed term Joint Venture beginning November 27, 2018 and ending November 30th, 2019 or as otherwise provided in this Agreement. Where the Joint Venture is entered for a fixed term and the Joint Venture continues after the expiration of that term then in the absence of an express new agreement, the rights and duties of the Participants remain the same as they were at the expiration of that term so far as those terms are consistent with a Joint Venture at will.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed withdrawal 'at any time.' The changed text restricts this, allowing withdrawal only after the 'fixed term' mentioned in Section 4. Section 4 defines this term. This creates a direct contradiction: a participant can withdraw 'at any time' (original) versus only after a specific date (changed). This introduces uncertainty and is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Dissolution Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, the Joint Venture may be dissolved only with the unanimous consent of all Participants.
perturbation.changed_text: Dissolution Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, the Joint Venture may be dissolved only with the consent of a majority of all Participants.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a direct contradiction. The original text states dissolution requires unanimous consent, but the modified text requires only a majority vote. This introduces uncertainty and could lead to disputes over whether the Joint Venture has been legally dissolved. Changing the vote to 'majority' directly contradicts with unanimous, which could then lead to in-text contradiction within the agreement, for instance Section 49.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The following list of actions will require the unanimous consent of all Participants: committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities or obligations totaling To Be Determined; and incurring single expenditures that exceed "To Be Determined".
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the requirement for dissolving the Joint Venture from 'unanimous consent' to 'majority consent'. The contradicted text explicitly states that 'committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities or obligations' requires unanimous consent. Changing the dissolution requirement to majority consent directly contradicts the provision requiring unanimous consent for committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities because dissolution would effectively allow the majority to circumvent the unanimous consent requirement. This is a strong in-text contradiction as defined by Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Actions Requiring Unanimous Consent of the Participants The following list of actions will require the unanimous consent of all Participants: committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities or obligations totaling To Be Determined; and incurring single expenditures that exceed "To Be Determined".
perturbation.changed_text: Actions Requiring Unanimous Consent of the Participants The following list of actions will require the consent of a majority of Participants: committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities or obligations totaling To Be Determined; and incurring single expenditures that exceed "To Be Determined".
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that certain actions, such as committing the Joint Venture to new liabilities or incurring large expenditures, require unanimous consent. By changing this to a majority vote, it directly contradicts the original requirement for unanimous consent. This contradiction could lead to internal disputes and legal challenges if a decision is made with a majority vote that would have previously required unanimity. The contradiction is explicit in terms of the voting requirements for specific actions. This creates conflict with section 35.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, the Joint Venture may be dissolved only with the unanimous consent of all Participants.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the voting requirement for specific actions from unanimous consent to majority consent. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that dissolution of the Joint Venture requires unanimous consent. The change creates a situation where the agreement specifies unanimous consent for dissolution but allows for potentially conflicting actions (committing to liabilities or incurring large expenditures) to be approved by only a majority, which could indirectly lead to dissolution without unanimous consent, therefore creating a strong contradiction.
